title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What is the Impact of Delay in filing for ITR?
Meta description: Thinking of delaying filing for income tax for just a little bit? Here’s what would happen if you miss filing your ITRs within the due date.
Keywords: income tax calculator, filing for income tax, itr delay penalty
Every year, individuals and non-individuals alike, are required to file their income tax returns (ITRs) on or before the 31st of July. This return that they would have to file would pertain to the previous financial year. For instance, if you’re filing for income tax returns on July 31, 2022, it would be for the income that you earned in the previous financial year, which would be 2021-2022.
If you’re an individual with no income or income that’s lower than the exemption limit, you won’t have to file any income tax returns. However, if you’re an individual whose income is above the exemption limit, then it is extremely important to file your income tax returns on time. Not doing so can lead you into a lot of trouble. Continue reading to find out more about what would happen if you delay filing your ITRs.
Impact of Delay in Filing for Income Tax Returns
The impact of any delay in filing your ITRs would be profound. You would not only have to contend with an ITR delay penalty levied on you, but would also face a few other consequences as well. Let’s take a more in depth look at what is likely to happen.
Penalties
Introduced in the financial year 2017-18, an ITR delay penalty of Rs. 5,000 would be levied on individuals who delay filing their income tax returns beyond 31st July, but file them before the 31st of December.
On the other hand, if there’s a further delay in filing ITRs beyond 31st December, the penalty would increase to Rs. 10,000.
The penalties tend to be lower if you’re a small tax payer. For instance, if you’re an individual whose total income is lower than Rs. 5 lakhs, the penalty for delaying the filing of ITRs would only be Rs. 1,000.
You wouldn’t be able to set off losses
A penalty isn’t the only consequence of delaying the filing of income tax returns. The provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961 allow you to set off losses that you incur in a year against profits that you make in subsequent years. This allows you to reduce the impact of income tax.
However, the benefit of carrying forward and setting off losses will no longer be available to you if you fail to file your income tax returns within the due date. This can lead you to miss out on reducing your income tax during the subsequent years.
Liable to pay interest for delaying the filing
In addition to the ITR delay penalty, the Income Tax Department may also levy interest for not filing your return. According to section 234A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, a simple interest of 1% (calculated on the tax liability) would be levied for each month of delay.
For instance, if your tax liability is Rs. 10,000 and you file your ITR only on January 31, 2022 as opposed to July 31, 2021, you would have to pay 1% of Rs. 10,000 for each month of delay as interest. This would come up to Rs. 600.
A delay in getting income tax refunds
If you’ve paid more tax to the income tax authorities than your actual tax liability, you are eligible to get a refund of the excess tax paid. However, to do that, filing for income tax returns is mandatory. Therefore, the longer you delay filing for income tax, the longer it would take to get your excess tax refunded back to you.
Conclusion
As you can see, there are multiple consequences that you would have to face, with the ITR delay penalty being just one of them. Therefore, it is a good idea to make sure that you do it on time and well within the due date.
When filling your ITR, remember to use an income tax calculator to do a simple check of whether the tax liability shown on the return is correct. This will ensure that you don’t make any mistakes while filing the return.
Here’s something that you should know. Although the general due date for filing income tax returns is the 31st of July of each year, the Income Tax Department occasionally tends to extend the due date depending on the existing circumstances. In such cases, the extended due date would apply.
